items.q: What is the significance of Duchamp's 'Fountain'?
items.correct: It challenged the definition of art - the artist's choice, not their skill, becomes the artwork.
items.random_wrong: It is a study of plumbing.
items.why: Students miss the conceptual point. Duchamp didn't make the urinal - he selected and presented it. The radical claim is that ART IS DESIGNATION, opening the door to all conceptual art.

items.q: What did Pollock mean by his drip paintings being 'in the painting'?
items.correct: He worked from all sides, making the act of painting central to the meaning.
items.misconception: He literally fell into wet paint.
items.why: Students take 'in the painting' literally. Pollock meant his BODILY ENGAGEMENT - working around and over the canvas on the floor - was integral to the work, not just the marks left behind.

items.q: What were Impressionist painters primarily trying to capture?
items.correct: The fleeting effects of light and atmosphere at a particular moment.
items.misconception: Their feelings or first impressions of a scene.
items.why: Students take 'impression' as personal feeling. Impressionism was about visual perception - how light actually appears on surfaces, painted quickly outdoors before light changed.

items.q: What is the typical word count range for the A-Level personal investigation written element?
items.why: It's 1000-3000 words. “100-500 words” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What term describes research drawn from books, articles and online sources?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Secondary research. “Primary research” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the maximum word count for the A-Level personal investigation written element under most exam boards?
items.why: The real figure is 3000 words. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
